title: John Jacob Wins!
author: James Silberman
orientation: Free the States reports the November 2020 victory of abolitionist John Jacob in Indiana HD93, framing his win as a milestone in which abolitionists are no longer merely converting incumbents but successfully unseating pro-life establishment Republicans to enter state legislatures with a mandate to immediately criminalize abortion.
audiences: Christian church member discerning political engagement

questions.question: What was the margin of John Jacob's victory in Indiana House District 93, and who did he defeat in the general election?
questions.answer: Jacob won with more than 65 percent of the vote against Democrat Angela Elliot, after first defeating incumbent Rep. Dollyne Sherman in the Republican primary.
questions.quote: Abolitionist John Jacob has been elected to the Indiana State house with more than 65 percent of the vote, defeating Democrat Angela Elliot.

questions.question: How does this race illustrate the divide between the pro-life establishment and the abolitionist movement?
questions.answer: Sherman, the incumbent Jacob unseated in the primary, carried the endorsement of Indiana Right to Life (the state's pro-life incrementalist organization), while Jacob was backed by abolitionist groups Abolish Abortion Indiana and Hoosiers for Life that demand total and immediate abolition.
questions.quote: Sherman was endorsed by the state’s pro-life incrementalist organization, Indiana Right to Life, while Jacob was endorsed by Abolish Abortion Indiana and Hoosiers for Life, organizations which advocate the total and immediate abolition of abortion.

questions.question: Why does Free the States consider Jacob's election (alongside Warren Hamilton's) historically significant for abolitionism?
questions.answer: Earlier abolitionist legislators, beginning with Oklahoma's Joseph Silk in 2016, had converted to abolitionism after taking office; Jacob and Hamilton are the first to run as abolitionists against incumbent anti-abolitionists and win their seats outright.
questions.quote: While a number of state legislators have converted to abolitionism in recent years beginning with Oklahoma State Senator Joseph Silk in 2016, Jacob and [Oklahoma State Senator-elect Warren Hamilton](https://freethestates.org/2020/11/warren-hamilton-wins/index.html) are the first abolitionists to challenge incumbent anti-abolitionists and enter the legislature as abolitionists.

questions.question: What concrete legislative agenda is Jacob expected to push when he arrives at the Indiana State House?
questions.answer: His top priority is criminalizing abortion by passing the Protection at Conception Act, the immediate-abolition bill that Rep. Curt Nisly has introduced every year since 2017 and which Jacob will now help advance.
questions.quote: John has repeatedly stated that his number one priority is to criminalize abortion by working to pass the Protection at Conception Act.

questions.question: How did Derin Stidd of Abolish Abortion Indiana characterize the kind of campaign Jacob ran?
questions.answer: Stidd credits a disciplined, voter-focused campaign for letting Jacob defeat both a pro-life establishment Republican and a pro-abortion Democrat, framing Jacob as a man of Christian principle and integrity.
questions.quote: He was able to unseat a pro-life establishment Republican in the primary, and a pro-abortion Democrat in the general election, because he ran a disciplined campaign that connected with the voters in his district.

questions.question: How does Jacob himself frame the work that lies ahead, and what does he ask of supporters?
questions.audience: Christian church member discerning political engagement
questions.answer: Jacob treats the win as a beginning rather than an end, casts the fight in spiritual terms drawn from Ephesians, and asks supporters to pray for him as he expects opposition from spiritual as well as political powers.
questions.quote: Please pray for me as I know there will be attacks and I realize that we do not struggle against flesh and blood, but against principalities, against powers, against the rulers of the darkness of this world, against spiritual wickedness in high places.

questions.question: What political message does Free the States believe Jacob's victory sends to the Republican establishment in Indiana?
questions.answer: It signals that the abolitionist movement now has the electoral muscle to remove sitting officeholders who refuse to legislate the immediate criminalization of abortion.
questions.quote: Jacob’s victory is a signal to the pro-life Republican establishment in Indiana that the Abolitionist Movement is now a political force in the state with the ability to remove people from office who will not legislate justly.

related.title: Using the A-Word, Indiana Abolitionist John Jacob Unseats Incumbent Pro-Life Rep.
related.reason: Companion article from June 2020 covering Jacob's primary win against Rep. Sherman, the prerequisite to this general-election victory.

related.title: Warren Hamilton Wins!
related.reason: Reports the parallel Oklahoma Senate victory of the other abolitionist explicitly named in this piece as a co-pioneer of unseating anti-abolitionist incumbents.

related.title: Reps. Curt Nisly, John Jacob Author the Bill to Abolish Abortion in Indiana
related.reason: Direct sequel showing Jacob, after taking office, co-authoring the Protection at Conception Act he campaigned on.

related.title: Indiana Pro-Life Leaders Kill Protection at Conception Act
related.reason: Background on the Indiana Right to Life-aligned establishment that Jacob's election aims to overcome on the same Nisly bill referenced here.

related.title: Watch - Of Giants & Heroes: Joseph Silk
related.reason: Profiles the 2016 Oklahoma senator this article cites as the first state legislator to convert to abolitionism, providing the lineage Jacob extends.

related.title: Disgraceful! Pro-Life Establishment Lies About Abolitionist Candidate Warren Hamilton
related.reason: Documents the same establishment-versus-abolitionist dynamic in Hamilton's parallel campaign that Jacob faced in Indiana.
